risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Individual patients were randomly allocated to one of the three treatment arms using computer generated randomised permuted blocks (with randomly varying block sizes of 9, 12, 15, and 18), after stratification on the basis of whether the patient was non-ambulatory ... and whether the patient fulfilled London ME criteria." Comment: This explicitly describes a computer-generated random allocation sequence with permuted blocks and stratification, which is a proper random method and is sufficient for Low risk.

Quote: "Details of eligible patients were provided by telephone to an independent randomisation service within two working days." Comment: This indicates that allocation was implemented through an independent randomisation service by telephone, which is an explicit concealment mechanism that would prevent the enrolling researcher from foreseeing the next assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The treatments are described in our protocol16 and in table 1." / "Patients in the pragmatic rehabilitation and supportive listening arms were free to consult their GP during the course of treatment." / "A programme of graded return to activity is designed collaboratively by the patient and the therapist" and "A listening therapy based on non-directive counselling..." Comment: This was an open, non-pharmacological 3-arm trial with clearly different active therapies and a usual-care control; the report does not state that participants or treating personnel were blinded. Given the nature of the interventions, participants and therapists would have known allocation, and the subjective outcomes (fatigue, physical functioning, sleep, anxiety/depression) could plausibly be influenced by that knowledge, so the dominant risk for performance bias is high.

Quote: "Assessments were performed by trained researchers blind to treatment allocation; any unblindings were recorded." Comment: The outcome assessors were explicitly blinded. The main outcomes (SF-36 physical functioning, Chalder fatigue scale, HADS, Jenkins sleep scale) were assessed by these blinded researchers; although these are largely patient-reported measures, the study states the assessments were performed by blinded researchers, reducing the chance that knowledge of allocation influenced outcome measurement.

Quote: "We aimed to recruit 360 patients... and allow for loss to follow-up of 23%. ... we revised our recruitment target to 288 in September 2006 when it became clear that our loss to follow-up was less than anticipated (13% at 70 weeks)." Comment: Attrition was modest overall and explicitly reported as 13% at 70 weeks, which is within the permissive low-risk threshold. The paper also reports ITT analysis and weighting adjustments for missing outcomes, and the follow-up rates by arm at 70 weeks (81/95, 90/101, 86/100) look reasonably balanced, so incomplete outcome data are unlikely to have biased the results.
